segments.s056-g241: به شادی is an adverbial prepositional phrase meaning 'in/with joy' placed before the verb for emphasis; here به (preposition) expresses manner ('-ly'/'with') and شادی is the abstract noun 'joy'. به شهر اندرون آمدند is a normal past-tense clause: the second به marks direction/into ('into the city'), اندرون is a literary word for 'interior/inner quarters', and آمدند is past plural 'they came/entered'.
segments.s056-g242: ابا here is a poetic/archaic form corresponding to با ('with') and introduces the manner/quality; پهلوانی is the abstract noun 'heroism/valour', and فزون آمدند is a compound predication meaning 'became more/increased' with آمدند in the past plural agreeing with the plural subject ('they grew/multiplied'). The clause states that their heroism increased.
segments.s056-g25: This is a wish clause headed by که ... باد with باد as the optative particle meaning “may … be.” بر is the preposition “upon/for” (classical usage: “may X be upon Y”), so بر سام یل = “for Sam the hero”; روز فرخنده = “auspicious day.” Word order is poetic (adjective after noun).
segments.s056-g26: Another optative clause ending in باد: دل بدسگالان او is a genitive phrase “the hearts of his ill‑omened ones / ill‑wishers” (او marks possession). کنده is a past participle meaning “torn/uprooted.” Together with باد it expresses a prayer: “may the hearts ... be torn out.”
segments.s056-g27: پس “then.” پرده تو = “your curtain” (تو as possessive/address to the addressee). در‌ای is an archaic/poetic imperative 'در آی' = “enter/come in,” addressed vocatively to نامجوی (the name follows). Word order places the vocative name at the end.
segments.s056-g28: یکی پور پاک آمد = “a certain pure son came.” یکی here = “a certain/one,” پور = “son/young man,” پاک = “pure/beautiful,” آمد = past “came.” از ماه‌روی is a genitive/adverbial “of moon‑faced,” describing him as moon‑faced (beautiful).
segments.s056-g29: تنش = تن + -ش (enclitic = his) “his body.” نقره سیم is a poetic compound meaning “silver‑colored / like silver.” و links to رخ چون بهشت = “and (his) face like paradise.” Adjectives and similes follow the nouns in poetic word order.
segments.s056-g30: بر او = “on him.” نبینی is the negative present/subjunctive (here rendered as a general/future‑oriented negative) of دیدن “to see,” meaning “you will not see / one does not see.” یک اندام زشت = “a single ugly feature/limb” (اندام used as ‘feature/figure’). Overall: “you will not see a single ugly feature on him.”
segments.s056-g31: This is a poetic verbless/nominal clause reordered: از آهو = “like/from a gazelle.” همان کش سپید است موی literally arranges as “the same pulled/lock white is (the) hair.” موی (hair) is described as سپید (white, here ‘white‑haired’). کش functions as a noun derived from ‘to pull’ (a ‘lock’/tress, poetic usage) and است is the copula “is,” so: “His hair is a white lock like from a gazelle.”
segments.s056-g32: چنین بود = “thus was / such was” with بود as past copula. بخش here means “portion / lot.” تو‑ای نامجوی is the vocative “O you, Namjui.” The whole line: “Such was your portion, Namjui.”
segments.s056-g49: چه گویم is a rhetorical interrogative phrase meaning "what shall I say" (چه + present-subjunctive/optative verb گویم). که introduces the content clause that follows (a subordinate clause or indirect question). The clause این بچه دیو چیست؟ is a direct question "is this child a demon?" with the copula چی + ست (چیست) forming the question "what/what is it?".
segments.s056-g50: پلنگ و دو رنگ است is a verbless-like descriptive clause: پلنگ (leopard) with و دو رنگ (and two‑coloured) predicates are linked to the copula است “is.” وگر نه is a fixed phrase meaning "and if not / otherwise," and پری‌ست is a fused adjective+copula meaning "(it) is a fairy" or "fairy‑like."
segments.s056-g51: از این ننگ is an ablative/causal phrase "because of this shame." بگذارم here functions as the speaker's verb meaning "I will leave/abandon" (first person present/future sense) and takes ایران‌زمین as its object: "I will abandon the land of Iran."
segments.s056-g52: نخواهم is negative of خواهم and here means "I will not / I do not wish" (first person). بر این بوم و بر آفرین states the locations the speaker refuses to remain on: بر + noun = "on/upon this soil and on Afarin" (آفرین here is a place/name in context, functioning as a location).
segments.s056-g53: بفرمود is an honorific/simple past verb meaning "he said/ordered." پس = "then/so." تاش here functions as a direct object (likely a proper name of the child in context) and برداشتند is the past plural verb "they took/lifted/removed." The sequence shows command → action.
segments.s056-g54: از آن بوم indicates source "from that land/soil" (از + آن + بوم). و بر دور بگذاشتند is a result clause: بر دور (at a distance/far away) + بگذاشتند (they placed/left) — they set it away at a distance. Word order is typical: source phrase then result action.
segments.s056-g55: به جایی "to a place" (به + جایی) introduces a destination. که سیمرغ را خانه بود is a relative/subordinate clause "where the Simurgh had a house/dwelling," with را marking سیمرغ as the (topical/definite) object and بود as the past copula "was/there was."
segments.s056-g56: بدان خانه is a contracted locative (بدان = به + آن) meaning "to that house." این خرد بیگانه بود is a copular clause: این خرد "this young one/this little being" + بیگانه "strange/alien" + بود (past copula) — "that house was a strange home for this child." Note: the segment contains the contraction بدَان; the analysis treats ب‑ as the locative prefix.
segments.s056-g65: که‌گر (archaic/poetic conditional) = 'if' (literally 'that if'); the clause that follows is a protasis. Word order is emphatic: من (subject) precedes تو را (marked object/beneficiary) and then the thing given خون دل ('the blood of the heart' = deep devotion/suffering) as the direct object; دادمی is an older past form meaning 'I gave' (first-person past).
segments.s056-g66: سپاس ایچ بر سرت ننهادمی is a negative perfect/past assertion: سپاس 'thanks'; ایچ 'not a thing/none' (intensifier 'not at all' / 'no'); بر سرت 'upon your head' (prepositional phrase where بر = on/upon and سرت = 'your head'); ننهادمی is the negative past form 'I did not place' (archaic/neatly contracted negation). Together: 'I placed no thanks upon your head.'
segments.s056-g67: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that/because'. تو خود 'you yourself' (خود = emphatic/reflexive). مرا (here split orthographically into م + را) is the object 'me' (را marks the definite object). دیده is the past participle 'seen' ('have seen'), so this clause says 'that you yourself have seen me'. و هم دلی appends 'and (have) shared my heart/are of one heart' — هم‌دلی = sympathy or being of one heart, here used as a predicate/noun that completes the clause.
segments.s056-g68: This is a conditional/result clause: دلم 'my heart' (subject) + بگسلد is an archaic/poetic imperfect/subjunctive form with the present-tense prefix ب- meaning 'would / might split'; the enclitic -گر (here attached to بگسلد‌گر) signals the 'if' condition ('گر' = 'if'). The condition follows: ز من بگسلی 'if you break away from me' (ز = from; من = me; بگسلی = 'you break' / 'you should break' — a verb form meaning 'to break away'). So: 'my heart would split if you were to part from me.'
segments.s056-g69: چو = 'when'. سیمرغ را بچه is an older/poetic way to express 'the Simorgh's chick' (را marks the definite/theme; the order is noun + را + appositive 'بچه'). شد is the past 'became' and گرسنه is the predicate adjective 'hungry'. So: 'when the Simorgh's chick became hungry...'.
segments.s056-g70: به پرواز بر شد is a light-verb idiom: به پرواز = 'into flight' and بر شد = 'rose up / took off' (بر as directional/adverbial + شد 'became/rose'). دمان از بنه is a postverbal adverbial: دمان (literally 'hem/edge' but here = 'from its nest/edge') + از بنه 'from the pistachio (bush/tree)'. The whole phrase: 'it took flight and rose up from the pistachio-bush.'
segments.s056-g71: یکی here means 'a certain / someone' (indefinite subject). شیرخواره is a compound meaning 'suckling' or 'nursling' (young animal fed on milk) and خروشنده is an adjective 'roaring/tumultuous'. دید is the simple past 'saw / was seen' (in epic poetry the subject/object relations can be compact; here 'one saw a roaring suckling').
segments.s056-g72: زمین را is the definite object 'the earth' (را marks the object). چو introduces a simile 'like/as'. دریای جوشنده = 'a sea (دریا) seething/boiling (جوشنده)'. The final دید repeats the past 'saw'. The line therefore likens the ground to a boiling sea as perceived by the observer.
segments.s056-g41: This is a conditional clause introduced by اگر (if). The subject من (I) is explicit; گناهی گران is a noun + adjective phrase ('a heavy/serious sin') with the adjective following the noun in classical word order. کرده‌ام is the perfect (compound past) 1st-person form 'I have done/committed', so the whole segment reads 'if I have committed a grave sin.'
segments.s056-g42: وگر ('or if') presents an alternative conditional. کیش آهرمن is 'the creed/sect of Ahriman'; آورده‌ام is again the perfect 1st-person 'I have brought/introduced'. In older/poetic Persian the verb can appear before or after its object; here the phrase means 'or if I have introduced the worship/creed of Ahriman.'
segments.s056-g43: به پوزش here means 'for the sake of pardon' (به + noun). مگر functions as an optative/particle in this classical line (roughly 'perhaps' / 'may it be that'), introducing the wish. کردگار جهان is a title phrase 'the Maker/Creator of the world' and is the agent of the hoped-for action.
segments.s056-g44: This is the rest of the optative clause. به من بر ببخشاید: بر marks the sense 'upon/over' and with ببخشاید (a present/subjunctive form meaning 'may he forgive') yields 'may he forgive me.' اندر نهان is an adverbial phrase 'in secret.' In classical Persian the verb ببخشاید expresses a wish here rather than a plain present tense.
segments.s056-g45: بپیچد is a present/poetic verb form 'twists/turns' placed before its subject (typical in older Persian). همی is a durative/emphatic particle ('still/continually'); تیره qualifies جانم ('my soul' with the possessive suffix -م), and ز شرم means 'from/because of shame.' Note the possessive suffix on جان (جانم) expresses 'my soul.'
segments.s056-g46: بجوشد (literally 'it boils') is used figuratively here — 'seethes' with emotion — and again precedes the locative phrase. همی gives durative aspect. در دلم is 'in my heart' (دلم = دل + possessive -م), and خون گرم 'hot/warm blood' is the subject that 'seethes' (بجوشد) in that place.
segments.s056-g47: چو is the temporal 'when'; آیند and پرسند are 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive forms ('they come' and 'they ask') typical of epic diction. و links the two verbs, and گردن‌کشان ('neck-cutters', i.e. 'rebels' or 'brigands') names the people who will come and inquire.
segments.s056-g48: چه گویم is a rhetorical question 'what shall I say' with گویم in the 1st-person subjunctive/optative. از indicates 'about' or 'concerning'; این بچه 'this child'; بدنشان is the noun بدن 'body' with the third-person plural suffix -شان, here marking 'to them'/'their relation' (i.e. 'to them, regarding the child's body'). Word order places the question word and verb up front for emphasis.
segments.s056-g57: نهادند is a past‑tense plural verb ('they placed/put'); بر is the preposition 'on/upon'; کوه is the noun 'mountain' as the place where something was placed. و joins the clauses ('and'); گشتند is a past‑tense plural verb 'they turned/returned' (here 'returned'); باز is an adverb 'again' or 'back', reinforcing the sense of returning. Word order is subject‑verb(s) omitted (they) + verb + loco‑phrase, then conjunction + verb + adverb.
segments.s056-g58: برآمد is a past/perfective verb 'arose/appeared'; بر is the preposition here introducing the temporal/occurrence phrase; این روزگاری دراز is a noun phrase 'this long period/time' (این = 'this', روزگاری = 'time/period', دراز = 'long'). Together the clause means that a long span of time then began or passed.
segments.s056-g59: چنان is a demonstrative/adverbial 'such/so'; پهلوان زاده is a compound noun meaning 'hero‑born' or 'a man of heroic birth' (پهلوان 'hero' + زاده 'born/offspring'); بی‌گناه is the adjective 'innocent' modifying the noun phrase. The order is demonstrative/adverb + noun phrase + postpositive adjective.
segments.s056-g60: ندانست is ن + دانست, a past negative verb meaning 'he did not know' or 'he could not tell/recognize'; رنگ here means 'color' or 'distinction'; سپید and سیاه are the adjectives 'white' and 'black' respectively; از links the pair as a comparison 'from' (i.e. 'white from black' = 'to distinguish white from black'). The clause literally says he did not know/recognize color, i.e. could not distinguish white from black.
segments.s056-g61: پدر is the subject 'the father'; مهر و پیوند are direct objects 'affection and bond/kinship'; بفگند is an older/poetic past form meaning 'cast/threw' or 'treated (as)'; خوار is a predicate adjective 'despised, lowly' describing the result. The clause means the father treated affection and ties as contemptible. Note the archaic form بفگند (from فگندن/فکندن) used in epic diction.
segments.s056-g62: جفا کرد is a past verbal phrase 'he did cruelty/wronged'; بر introduces the object affected 'against/on' and is used with کودک شیرخوار 'the suckling child / nursing infant' (کودک 'child', شیرخوار 'suckling, nursing'). The clause says he committed injustice/cruelty toward the infant.
segments.s056-g63: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain / someone'; داستان زد is an idiomatic expression 'told/spoke a tale' where زد functions as a light verb for 'telling' (poetic usage). بر این نره شیر is a prepositional phrase 'about/on this male‑lion' (نره 'male (animal)', شیر 'lion' here). The whole segment introduces a recounted story concerning that male lion.
segments.s056-g64: کجا is an interrogative/adverb 'where' (here introducing the subordinate clause of the tale); بچه را is the definite object 'the child' (را marks the object); کرده is a perfect/past participial form 'had made / had done' used as the main verb; بد is an adverb 'badly' (here functioning to modify the following compound) and شیر سیر is a compound meaning 'satiated/full with milk' (شیر 'milk' + سیر 'sated'). Grammatically this gives a clause asking/relating the place where he had made the child milk‑sated. (Note: the line is compact/poetic and allows some ambiguity in how بد modifies the phrase.)
segments.s056-g9: از introduces the source or cause ('from' / 'on the basis of'); آن ماهش is a poetic expression where ماه (with enclitic -ش 'her') means a beautiful person ('that moon of hers'). امید فرزند is the nominative phrase 'hope/expectation of a child' serving as the subject, and بود is the past copula 'was'. Word order is emphatic/poetic (prepositional phrase fronted, then the subject and copula).
segments.s056-g10: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause describing that expected child ('who' / 'that'); خورشید‌چهر is a compound adjective 'sun-faced' and و simply links two adjectives, برومند is a descriptive adjective 'noble/prosperous', and بود is again the past copula 'was'. The clause gives attributes of the hoped-for child.
segments.s056-g11: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/of', giving origin; the two names سام نریمان form a genitive or linked reference to ancestry or source. هم follows them to mean 'also', and او is the subject pronoun 'he'. بار داشت is a light verb construction where بار ('burden/load') is the noun and داشت is 'had/held' in past — together 'he bore a burden'. The order (prepositional phrase → modifier → subject → predicate) is typical of classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s056-g12: ز again introduces cause ('from/owing to'); بار گران is a noun + adjective ('heavy burden'); تنش is 'his body' (تن + enclitic ش). آزار داشت is another light-verb phrase: آزار (harm/distress) + داشت ('had') = 'suffered' or 'was afflicted'. The clause explains the consequence of the burden.
segments.s056-g13: ز مادر جدا شد is a short clause: ز + مادر = 'from the mother', جدا شد is the past passive/unstated-subject construction 'was separated' or 'separated himself' (شد = 'became/was separated'). بر آن چند روز is a temporal phrase 'for those few days' (بر used as 'for'/'during'), with چند روز = 'several/a few days'. The sequence gives duration immediately after the verb, typical in Persian.
segments.s056-g14: نگاری is a poetic noun meaning 'a beauty / beloved' (here referring to the child); چو is the simile 'like/as', خورشید is 'sun', and گیتی‌فروز is a compound adjective 'world‑illuminating' (literally 'making the world shine'). The short noun phrase is a descriptive apposition to the subject in the previous clause.
segments.s056-g15: به چهره is an adverbial/locative phrase 'in/with regard to his face/appearance'; چنان بود is 'was such/so', where بود is past copula. تابنده is a participial adjective 'shining/radiant' and شید is a poetic epithet 'beloved' or 'love‑mad' used as a descriptive noun/adjective. The clause characterizes his visage.
segments.s056-g16: ولیکن is the conjunction 'but/yet'; همه موی 'all his hair' (همه = all, موی = hair), بودش is the copula with enclitic pronoun (-ش 'his') meaning 'was his' or simply 'was', and سپید describes the hair as 'white/grey'. The clause gives a contrastive fact about his appearance (radiant face but white hair).
segments.s056-g121: This is a prepositional/timeal phrase followed by an imperative-like clause: 'به یزدان کنون' = 'to God now'. 'سوی پوزش گرای' = 'turn toward pardon/seek pardon' where سوي + object gives the direction and گرای here is an archaic/poetic verbal form meaning 'turn/ incline' (an imperative or hortative). The word order places the destination (به یزدان) and time (کنون) before the verb phrase 'سوی پوزش گرای'.
segments.s056-g122: که begins a subordinate/explanatory clause: 'that/for'. اوی است = 'he is' with the archaic pronoun اوی (subject) followed by the copula است. بر نیکویی رهنمای = predicate: رهنمای (guide) governed by the preposition بر indicating direction/goal ('toward goodness'). The whole clause gives the reason: 'for he is a guide toward goodness.'
segments.s056-g123: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. The clause 'شب تیره شد' is straightforward: 'the night became dark' (subject + adjective + past verb شد). 'رای خواب آمدش' follows: رای (intention/plan/desire) + خواب (sleep) forms 'the desire/intention to sleep', and آمدش (came to him) uses the enclitic -ش to mark the experiencer ('to him').
segments.s056-g124: از introduces the source: 'from (his) thought'. The phrase 'از اندیشه دل شتاب آمدش' literally 'from thinking the heart haste came to him' — a compact poetic order where اندیشه (thought) is the source and شتاب آمدش ('haste came to him') is the resultant mental state. Note the inversion common in Persian poetry: verb at the end and the experiencer marked by -ش.
segments.s056-g125: چنان دید در خواب = 'he thus saw in a dream' (چنان modifies دید). کز کوه هند is a relative/prepositional phrase: کز here functions as 'from which' or 'from' introducing the origin (کوه هند = the mountain of India). The relative phrase attaches to what he saw.
segments.s056-g126: درفشی is the noun-object 'a banner' (poetic -ی ending). برافراشتندی is an archaic past plural/past-form meaning 'they raised/erected' (the older epic third-person plural). بلند is a postpositive adjective 'tall/high' describing the banner; Persian commonly places such adjectives after the noun in poetry.
segments.s056-g127: جوانی پدید آمدی = 'a youth appeared' (جوانی subject; پدید آمدی is an older/poetic form meaning 'appeared'/'came forth'). خوب‌روی is a descriptive adjective 'fair-faced/handsome' placed after the verb phrase, qualifying the youth. The verb form آمدی is archaic but functions as past tense.
segments.s056-g128: سپاهی گران = 'heavy/valuable soldiers' where گران is an adjective giving force/valor ('mighty'/'formidable'). از پس پشت اوی is a compressed locative: از پس = 'from behind/of the rear', پشت = 'back', and اوی is the archaic possessive 'his', placed after the noun; together 'from behind his back' or 'coming up from his rear.'
segments.s056-g17: This is a temporal clause: پسر (subject) + چون (classical 'as/when') introduces the time of birth. ز مادر is an ablative ('from/by the mother') using the poetic preposition ز, بر آن گونه is a prepositional phrase 'in/after that manner,' and زاد is a past verb 'was born/was begotten' (archaic/intransitive use). Word order is typical of older Persian poetry (subject + temporal marker + ablative + manner + past verb).
segments.s056-g18: نکردند is the past-plural negative (ن + کردند) 'they did not do/perform.' یاد کردن (here with یاد as a noun) means 'to remember/mention'; the construction places the duration یک هفته immediately after the verb (poetic word order) to mean 'for a week.' بر + proper name (بر سام) marks the target of the remembering/mentioning. So syntactically: 'they did not (یاد) [mention/remember] Sam for one week.'
segments.s056-g19: A noun phrase: شبستان (bedchamber/inner room) followed by the modifying phrase آن نامور پهلوان ('that renowned champion/hero'). آن is the demonstrative; نامور modifies پهلوان. This is a simple nominal phrase describing location.
segments.s056-g20: همه پیش is 'everyone before / in the presence of'; پیش governs the following noun phrase. آن introduces the noun phrase آن خرد کودک نوان, where خرد here means 'small/little' and کودک نوان is a compound describing a newly born/young child (poetic stacked modifiers after the demonstrative). The phrase as a whole means 'all before that little/newborn child.'
segments.s056-g21: کسی is 'someone/anyone' and here begins a clause. سام یل را uses the direct-object marker را on the name+epithet 'Sam the hero' — in older prose/poetry this can also mark a topicalized phrase. نیارست is an archaic/polished past form (negative/derived here in context) placed before the reporting verb گفت; the whole phrase functions as background to the speech that follows. Grammatically, the verb گفت ('said') is the reporting verb that introduces the following content clause (که ...).
segments.s056-g22: که is the complementizer 'that' introducing reported speech. فرزند پیر is 'the child (of) the elder/old man' (پیر = old). آمد is simple past 'came.' از introduces the source/parentage 'from/of' (از خوب‌جفت = 'of/from a well-matched spouse'); خوب‌جفت is a compound adjective/noun meaning 'well-matched (mate/spouse).'
segments.s056-g23: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain / one'; دایه is the noun 'wet‑nurse'. بودش is the past copula بود with the enclitic -ش (to him/her / for him/her) — here functioning like 'there was / she was (to him)'. به کردار شیر is an adverbial phrase: به + کردار (conduct/guise) + شیر (lion) = 'in the manner/guise of a lion' (به used adverbially to mark likeness).
segments.s056-g24: Word order is poetic: بر پهلوان ('toward/upon the champion') is a prepositional phrase placed before the verb. اندر آمد is the past verb 'came/entered'. دلیر is an adjectival predicate ('brave'), placed after the verb for emphasis — so the clause reads 'came upon the champion, brave' = 'boldly approached the champion.'
segments.s056-g129: به دست چپش is a prepositional phrase meaning “in/at his left hand”; چپش is راست/چپ + possessive suffix -ش (“his left”). بر introduces what is placed on/at that location and precedes the noun mobiled in inverted poetic order; the copula (‘was’) is omitted (typical in epic Persian). یکی موبدی = “a certain mobad/priest.”
segments.s056-g130: سوی راستش = “to/at his right (side)”: سوی is the directional preposition and راستش is ‘right’ with the possessive -ش. نامور بخردی is an adjectival phrase: نامور “renowned” and بخردی (“wisdom”/“wise”) here means “famed for wisdom” or simply “a famed wise (man)”; again the noun (man) and copula are left implicit by poetic economy.
segments.s056-g131: این یکی پیش سام آمدی زان دو مرد is compacted word order: یکی (“one/a certain one”) is the subject, پیش سام (“in the presence of Sam” / before Sam) marks the address, آمدی is the past verb “came.” زان دو مرد = “of those two men” (زان = ‘of/from that’), so the whole means “one of those two men came before Sam.”
segments.s056-g132: زبان بر گشادی is the idiom “to open one’s tongue” = to begin speaking; بر here marks the object of that action (i.e. toward speech). گشادی is a past/participial form (‘opened’) used poetically. به گفتار سرد = “with/into a stern speech”: به here marks the manner (‘with’), گفتار “speech,” and سرد “stern/cold” describes the tone.
segments.s056-g133: که‌ای here is a vocative/exclamatory address (“O you…”); مرد is the addressee “man.” The two following modifiers describe him: بی‌باک “fearless” and ناپاک رای literally “unclean/immoral in counsel” (ناپاک modifies رای, ‘counsel/intent’). The order places the epithets immediately after the vocative for emphasis.
segments.s056-g134: دل و دیده شسته is a nominative phrase where دل “heart” and دیده “eye” are coordinated and governed by the participle شسته “washed/washed away.” ز introduces the source/reason (“of/from”), here ز شرم خدای = “of the shame before God” (i.e. they have been deprived of shame toward God). The copula is again omitted; the construction is accusative-like but functions predicatively in poetry.
segments.s056-g135: تو را at the start topicalizes/emphasizes “you” (را as a topic/definite object marker used here for emphasis). دایه‌گر مرغ is a sarcastic predicate: “a bird for a nurse” (i.e. ‘you were nursed by a bird’). شاید همی = “perhaps indeed” (شاید ‘perhaps’; همی is an archaic emphatic/adverbial particle).
segments.s056-g136: پس این پهلوانی چه باید همی is a rhetorical question: پس “then/so,” این پهلوانی “this (so-called) heroism,” چه باید “what is the use / what ought (it) to do?” همی again is an archaic emphatic particle. The verb باید is modal (‘ought/should’), making the line challenge the value of his supposed heroism.
segments.s056-g33: فرود آمد is a two-part past verb meaning 'alighted/dismounted' (فرود + آمد). The prepositional phrase از تخت سام ('from Sam's throne') follows and supplies the source; in classical poetry the ezāfe linking تخت and سام is often omitted. سوار is an appositive noun 'the rider/horseman' identifying the one who dismounted.
segments.s056-g34: به پرده درآمد is a verbal phrase where به + noun (پرده 'curtain/tent flap') + درآمد ('entered' — here used as a past action) means 'entered into the tent/curtain'. سوی نو بهار is a directional phrase 'toward the new/fresh spring'; نو modifies بهار. Word order follows Persian: direction/goal after the verb phrase.
segments.s056-g35: چو here is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. فرزند را دید is a simple past clause ('he saw the son') with را marking the definite object. مویش سپید is a nominative/adjectival comment ('his hair white' = 'whose hair was white'); مویش contains the possessive suffix -ش 'his'.
segments.s056-g36: ببود is an archaic past copula 'was' (literary). از جهان سر به سر ناامید is a prepositional/idiomatic predicate: از جهان 'of/from the world' (i.e. with respect to life), سر به سر 'completely/utterly' (an emphatic idiom), ناامید 'hopeless'. Together: 'he was utterly hopeless of the world.'
segments.s056-g37: سوی آسمان is a directional prepositional phrase 'toward the sky/heaven'. سر برآورد is a literary past construction meaning 'he lifted/raised his head' (سر 'head' + برآورد 'raised'). راست functions adverbially here as 'straight' or 'directly'.
segments.s056-g38: ز is the poetic form of از 'from' or 'to' in compounds. ز دادآور آنگاه places the action 'then' with reference to the 'دادآور' ('bringer of justice' — a lofty epithet, here the addressee). فریاد خواست is a fixed literary collocation meaning 'cried out' or 'uttered a cry' (فریاد 'cry' + خواست 'he wanted/sought' used as the past tense verb forming the action).
segments.s056-g39: که‌ای here functions as a vocative/relative 'O you who...' (classical/poetic usage of که + ای). برتر از introduces a comparative 'superior to / better than', and کژی و کاستی are the two nouns being compared ('crookedness/wrongness' and 'deficiency/shortcoming'). The copula ('are') is omitted as is common in poetry.
segments.s056-g40: بهی here means 'better/good' and Zان فزاید (زان = 'from that/than that') + فزاید (3rd person singular present — used optatively/wishfully in poetry here 'may ... increase/become more') forms a wish: 'may something better than that increase.' The relative clause که تو خواستی ('that you wished') specifies what is being wished for; خواستی is past tense 'you wished'.
segments.s056-g1: کنون is an adverb at the sentence front meaning “now.” پرشگفتی (from پرشگفت) functions as an attributive adjective/noun ‘marvelous/astonishing’ modifying the following noun. یکی precedes داستان as an indefinite/number word meaning ‘a certain/one,’ so the phrase reads literally “now an astonishing one tale.”
segments.s056-g2: بپیوندم is the poet’s 1st‑person verb ‘I attach/relate’ (classical b‑prefix + پیوندم) — here used to mean ‘I append/relate.’ از marks the source ‘from,’ گفته is a verbal noun meaning ‘saying/report’ and باستان modifies it as ‘ancient,’ so the whole segment gives the source of the tale (“from ancient reports”).
segments.s056-g3: نگه کن is an imperative pair ‘look!’ (نگه ‘gaze’ + کن imperative ‘do’). که introduces the content clause. مر + سام + را is a nominal group: مر is an enclitic (first‑person/possessive/to‑me), سام is the proper name, and را is the definite/object marker, so روزگار (the following word) is established as the agent/topic (‘fate/time’).
segments.s056-g4: This clause contains the exclamatory question/remark: چه (‘what/how’) + بازی (‘game/trick’) + نمود (past verb ‘did/played’). نمود is followed by a vocative sequence (ای … پسر) — ‘ای’ as vocative addressing پسر ‘O son.’ گوش دار is an idiomatic imperative ‘give ear/listen’ (گوش 'ear/attention' + دار, an archaic verbal form meaning ‘hold/have’); note دار here is an archaic verbal element, not the noun meanings listed in the lemma data.
segments.s056-g5: نبود is the past negative copula ‘there was not/was not’; ایچ is an emphatic negative ‘any/at all’; فرزند is the noun ‘child/offspring.’ مر سام را is the noun phrase for the person affected (مر as an enclitic linked to سام, then را as the object/contrast marker) — the literal sense is that Sam had no child at all.
segments.s056-g6: دلش is the possessed noun ‘his heart’ (دل + enclitic ش = his); بود is the past copula ‘was.’ جوینده کام is a nominal phrase ‘seeker of desire’ (جوینده modifies کام), and را again marks or emphasizes the predicate. Word order: possessed noun + copula + predicate noun phrase.
segments.s056-g7: نگاری is a noun that can mean ‘portrait’ or ‘beloved image.’ بد here is an archaic/poetic modifier (in classical usage it can function as an intensifier yielding a positive sense like ‘fair/beautiful’) — the simple preposition اندر = ‘in/within’ introduces the locative phrase, شبستان = ‘bedchamber/inner chamber,’ and اوی is the enclitic pronoun ‘his/her’ referring back to the person described. The whole segment sets location and the pictured person.
segments.s056-g8: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from/of’ used in simile/metaphor. گلبرگ رخ داشت literally ‘had a face of a petal’ i.e. ‘her face was like a flower petal’ (داشت used here as a possessive/copis form ‘had/possessed’). و links the two parallel metaphors; the second clause likewise states ز مشک موی ‘hair of musk’ — an image saying her hair was (like) musk.
segments.s056-g225: فرود آمد is a past action meaning 'he descended' (literal 'came down'); از کوه is a prepositional phrase 'from the mountain' placed after the verb. The conjunction و links this clause to the next, and بالای خواست uses بالا (here functioning as a nominal/oblique 'an upper/over-garment') plus the past verb خواست 'he wanted'.
segments.s056-g226: همان جامه is a noun phrase 'that same garment/robe' (همان pointing back emphatically). خسرو آرای is a compound attributive: خسرو (Khosrow) + آرای (ornamenting/ornament) describing the robe as associated with or fit for Khosrow. خواست again is the past verb 'he wanted/desired', repeating the action for emphasis.
segments.s056-g227: سپه is the subject 'the army/host' and takes the plural past verb آمدند 'they came' (common in Persian to treat collectives with a plural verb). یک‌سره is an adverb 'straightaway/completely' modifying the motion; پیش سام is a prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of Sam' placed before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s056-g228: گشاده دل و شادکام are two coordinate predicates describing the manner/state of those who came: گشاده‌دل literally 'open-hearted' (an adjective/adnominal phrase) and شادکام 'fortunate/joyful'. آمدند repeats the plural past verb 'they came', so the adjectives modify how they arrived.
segments.s056-g229: تبیره‌زنان (pl.) is the subject 'the axemen' and بردند is the past transitive verb 'they carried/brought'. پیش here functions adverbially 'before/in front of (someone implied)' and پیل is the direct object 'an elephant' that they bring forward.
segments.s056-g230: برآمد is an intransitive past verb 'appeared/rose' (something came into view). یکی is an indefinite 'one' or 'a certain one' introducing the elephant as a single huge creature; گرد here functions adjectivally/adverbially to indicate roundness/magnitude ('about/round'), followed by a simile مانند نیل 'like the Nile' to stress the animal's vastness.
segments.s056-g231: خروشیدن is an infinitive/verbal noun 'the sounding/roaring' used as the main verbal idea ('there was the sounding'); کوس is the instrument/subject 'the war‑drum(s)'. با introduces the instrument accompaniment 'with' and کره‌نای names the small reed flute(s) that accompany the drums.
segments.s056-g232: همان ties back as 'the same' (referring to previously mentioned ornaments); زنگ زرین is a noun phrase 'golden bells' (زنگ here 'ringing/bell' qualified by زرین 'golden'). و links هندی درای, where هندی 'Indian' qualifies and درای (from دارا) means 'possessing' or 'rich/ornamented' — together indicating Indian (eastern) ornaments or richly adorned Indian pieces accompanying the sound.
segments.s056-g233: سواران همه نعره برداشتند is a compact clause: subject (سواران همه, “all the riders”) + verbal phrase (نعره برداشتند). برداشتند is past-tense, 3rd-person-plural; here it functions idiomatically meaning “raised” or “let out” (a shout), not literally “took away.” Word order is typical Persian S–V after giving the subject first for emphasis.
segments.s056-g234: بدان خرمی راه بگذاشتند consists of the locative/goal phrase (بدان = به + آن, “to/with that”) + manner/quality (خرمی, “merriment/joy”) and the verbal compound راه بگذاشتند (راه + بگذاشتند). راه بگذاشتند is an idiom meaning “set out on the road” or “took the way”; بگذاشتند is past 3rd-plural. The prepositional-looking ب + دان here together mark “to/with that.”
segments.s056-g235: چو اندر هوا شب علم برگشاد is a temporal clause introduced by چو (here “when”). اندر هوا شب literally orders elements poetically: اندر هوا شب = “at night in the air/sky” (in classical poetry هوا can mean the sky/atmosphere), then علم برگشاد = “the banner/standard opened/unfurled.” برگشاد is perfective/past participial sense “opened/unfurled.”
segments.s056-g236: شد آن روی رومیش زنگی نژاد is the main clause: شد (past of شدن) functions as the intransitive predicate meaning “happened/appeared.” آن روی = “that face,” رومیش (روم + ایش) marks origin/association with Rome/Romans, and زنگی نژاد is a compound attributive meaning “of Zangi stock / dark-skinned lineage.” Word order is predicate-final typical Persian, with modifiers following the head noun.
segments.s056-g237: بر آن دشت هامون فرود آمدند is literal: بر آن دشت هامون = “upon that plain (called) Hamun,” فرود آمدند is past 3rd-plural of آمدن with فرود as noun/adverbial meaning “descent/landing,” together meaning “they descended/upon it they came down.” The preposition بر marks direction/position “upon/on.”
segments.s056-g238: بخفتند و یکبار دم بر زدند has two coordinated verbs: بخفتند (past, 3rd-pl. of خفتن) means “they slept/lay down,” and یکبار دم بر زدند follows with و linking “and.” یکبار here is adverbial (“at once / with one stroke”), دم literally “breath/moment” is used idiomatically (e.g. a single blast or signal), and زدند is past 3rd-pl. of زدن used here in the sense “to sound/perform” (to blow or strike to make a signal).
segments.s056-g239: چو بر چرخ گردان درفشنده شید is a temporal/setting clause: چو “when,” بر چرخ گردان = “upon the revolving wheel/sky” (چرخ = sky/wheel, گردان = revolving), درفشنده = “standard-bearer/banner‑waver.” شید is a short epithet attached to درفشنده (poetic epithet that can mean “beloved/bright”); the clause is elliptical/poetic, setting the scene before the next clause. Verbal element is omitted here and implied in the following segment.
segments.s056-g240: یکی خیمه زد از حریر سپید: یکی (a single/one) + خیمه زد is an idiom meaning “pitched a tent” (زد here is past of زدن used idiomatically as ‘set up/strike [a tent peg]’). از حریر سپید (“of white silk”) is the material/appearance phrase following خیمه and modifies it. Word order: determiner/number → noun → verb → material phrase.
segments.s056-g177: روا باشد is an impersonal formula meaning “it is fitting/allowed,” with باشد in the subjunctive/optative sense. اکنون is a temporal adverb (“now”) placed before the subordinate clause; که introduces that clause. بردارمت is an archaic first‑person verb phrase “I will take/lift you off,” where -مت is an attached 2nd‑person object (you).
segments.s056-g178: بی‌آزار is an adverbial/adjectival phrase “harmless, without harm.” نزدیک او is a locative phrase “near him/her,” with او as the third‑person pronoun. آرمت is a first‑person verb (“I rest / I will repose”) in an older/poetic form; the construction places manner and location before the verb (adverb + locative + verb).
segments.s056-g179: به + noun (به سیمرغ) marks the object/direction “to/at the Simurgh.” بنگر is the imperative “look!” or “behold.” که introduces the reported clause. دستان (literally “hands,” but often metaphorical “tales/efforts” depending on context) is the subject of the following clause; چه is the interrogative/relative “what,” and گفت here functions as a past‑tense verb “said.”
segments.s056-g180: که continues the subordinate clause begun above. سیر is adjectival/nominal ‘sated’ or ‘full,’ used predicatively. آمدستی is an archaic second‑person past form “you came” (Thou camest / you have come). همانا is an emphatic particle “verily / indeed.” ز introduces the source “from,” and جفت here means “pair / mate,” so the clause says the addressee has come away sated from its mate.
segments.s056-g181: نشیم is a jussive/subjunctive form meaning “let me sit / may I sit” (an older poetic form). تو is the topical/contrastive pronoun “you.” رخشنده is an adjective “radiant/bright,” and گاه here functions as “place/occasion” (poetic use). من است places the possessor after the predicate: “you are my radiant place.”
segments.s056-g182: دو پر is a noun phrase “two feathers / the two wings” (numeral + noun). تو marks possession (“your”). فر is the technical epic term for divine/royal glory or splendour. کلاه من است is a predicative construction “is the cap/crown of me,” so the whole line says the addressee’s two feathers are the glorious crown of the speaker.
segments.s056-g183: چنین داد پاسخ is a compact past narrative clause “he gave such an answer” (چنین + داد + پاسخ). که‌گر is an archaic fused particle (که + گر) meaning “if” or “should (you)”; it introduces a conditional/subordinate clause. تاج و گاه pairs “crown and seat/throne” as the objects of that conditional.
segments.s056-g184: ببینی is a present/subjunctive second‑person form “you see / you should behold,” used here under the conditional just introduced. و simply links the following noun phrase. رسم کیانی means “the Kayanian (i.e., royal) custom/rite” (کیانی = Kayanian), and کلاه (“hat”) is the object/theme — together pointing to the Kayanian ceremonial hat as part of what one might behold.
segments.s056-g169: از این بر شدن is a temporal/locative phrase: از این = 'from here/at this (moment)' and بر شدن (poetic) = 'to rise, to take off'; together 'before/when I rise from here'. بنده را uses بنده as a humble self-reference ('your servant' = 'me') and را is the definite-object/contrast marker attached to it. دست‌گیر is a verbal/adjectival root meaning 'take hold / seize / be a helper' and here functions as an imperative/request addressed to the addressee: 'take hold (of me)'.
segments.s056-g170: مر is the dative/clitic 'to me/for me' (benefactive). این پر گنه را = 'this sinful feather' with پر 'feather' + گنه 'sin' as a descriptive phrase and را marking the object. تو (you) is the explicit subject, اندر 'within/in' is a locative particle, and پذیر (poetic/archaic) functions as an imperative/verb root 'accept, receive' — together: 'you receive/accept this sinful feather into yourself (for me)'.
segments.s056-g171: چنین گفت = 'thus said' (چنین 'thus/so' + گفت, reported speech marker). سیمرغ is the speaker (the mythical bird). با پور سام = 'to/with the son of Sam' where با here marks the addressee and پور سام is a patronymic 'son of Sam'. Overall: 'Thus the Simurgh said to Sam's son.'
segments.s056-g172: که‌ای here introduces the quoted address and functions vocatively/'so that' (poetic: 'O...'). دیده is used as a term of endearment 'my eye' (vocative). رنج نشیم is a negative subjunctive/optative: نشیم = 'may we not / let us not be' (poetic form), so رنج نشیم = 'let us not suffer/be grieved'. و کنام joins with a verbless/elliptical clause: کنام 'the lap/bosom' implies 'and (let me) place/keep (you) in my bosom' — poetic ellipsis is common here.
segments.s056-g173: This is a string of epithets in apposition: پدر سام = 'father of Sam' (head noun), followed by a series of descriptive nouns (یل پهلوان جهان) that further identify him: یل 'hero, brave one', پهلوان 'champion', جهان 'of the world' (postpositive modifier). Persian permits stacking epithets without extra particles.
segments.s056-g174: سرافرازتر is the comparative/adjectival form 'more honored/loftier'. کس here denotes 'person/one' (used generically). میان مهان = 'among the guests' (میان = among; مهان = guests). The phrase means 'more exalted than anyone among guests' — the comparative relation is implied rather than explicitly using 'از'.
segments.s056-g175: بدین = 'to/at this' (demonstrative + preposition). کوه 'mountain' is the locative place. فرزند جوی is a compound: فرزند 'child/descendant' and جوی here functions as an adjectival/participial 'seeking/searching' (or 'of a stream' in other contexts), producing 'the searching son'/'the child in search'. آمدست is an archaic perfect/present-perfect 'has come' (poetic past/perfect). Word order places location and descriptor before the verb, typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s056-g176: تو را is a topical/emphatic dative 'for you' or 'to you'. نزد او = 'near him/in his presence' (نزد = near/in the presence of; او = he). آب روی is an adjectival compound meaning 'water-faced' i.e. 'fair-faced, beautiful-faced' (a conventional epithet). آمدست again = 'has come/arrived'. The clause says 'a fair-faced one has come to you near him' (poetic ordering makes the beneficiary/topic 'to you' appear at the front).
segments.s056-g81: ببردش is a classical past form with the verbal prefix بـ plus بردن and the enclitic ـش (object 'him/it'): 'he carried/took him.' دمان here means the 'skirt' or 'foothill' (i.e. the lower slope of a mountain). تا ... به ... کوه (تا + به + اسمِ مکان) expresses direction/extent 'as far as/to Mount Alborz.' Word order is verb + object/enclitic, then location phrase.
segments.s056-g82: که is a relative/connector 'where/that.' بودش is بود (past 'was') with enclitic ـش referring back to the person/place: 'was there' or 'there was for him.' بدانجا is به + آنجا 'to / at that place.' کنام is an archaic noun 'abode/seat' (literally 'lap/bosom' used for dwelling). و گروه coordinates 'and company/retinue.' The clause describes the location where his abode and followers were.
segments.s056-g83: سوی بچگان برد: سوی + noun = 'toward the children.' برد here is a narrative verb 'carried/bore' (past). تا introduces purpose/result 'so that,' and بشکرند is the subjunctive/pluperfective-form sense 'they might thank' (بشکرند = به + شکرن in classical usage = 'give thanks'). The construction expresses motion with a purpose.
segments.s056-g84: بدان is a compound prepositional idea (به + آن) 'at/to that.' ناله زار is a noun phrase 'pitiable lament' (ناله = lament, زار = wretched/pitiable). او is the 3rd-person pronoun 'his/her.' ننگرند is نفی + نگرند (present/subjunctive form of نگریستن) meaning 'they do not look / they do not behold.' The clause says they do not look upon his wretched lament.
segments.s056-g85: ببخشود is a classical past verb (بـ + بخشود) meaning 'he granted/forgave/gave.' یزدان is the subject 'God.' نیکی‌دهش is an epithet/compound 'giver of good' or 'bounteous one' used appositionally with یزدان ('Yazdān, the giver of good'). The clause states that God bestowed (grace/life/favor).
segments.s056-g86: کجا here introduces a clause 'so that/where' (classical use linking result or place). بودنی is an abstract noun 'being/existence.' داشت is past 'had' (he/it had). اندر = 'in/within.' بوش in context means 'in her bosom/chest' (archaic spelling/pronunciation referring to the interior/bosom); it marks the locus where that being existed. Together: 'so that he had existence within her bosom.'
segments.s056-g87: نگه کرد is a light-verb construction: نگه (gaze/watch) + کرد (did) = 'looked' or 'kept watch.' سیمرغ is the subject 'the Simurgh.' با بچگان can mean 'with the children' or 'toward/at the children' (here 'at the children'). The phrase indicates the Simurgh's act of looking/regarding the children.
segments.s056-g88: بر آن خرد = 'upon that small one' (بر = upon, آن خرد = that little/young one). خون is the subject of the next verb 'blood.' از دو دیده = 'from (her) two eyes' (از = from, دو دیده = two eyes). چکان is a verbal/participial form 'dripped/was dripping' (classical poetic verb meaning 'to drip, let fall drops'). The clause pictures blood trickling from her two eyes onto the small one.
segments.s056-g73: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. خاراش is خارا + enclitic -ش ('its flint/rock'); the phrase is elliptical (copula omitted): 'from its flints (was) a cradle', then و links the next predicate 'and the earth (was) his nurse.' دایه is the predicate noun ‘nurse’ and خاک names the nurse as the earth.
segments.s056-g74: تن is the subject 'the body'. از جامه دور is a small predicative phrase: از + جامه ('from garment') + دور ('removed/away') = 'without clothing, stripped'. و links the next clause. لب از شیر پاک uses از to indicate source ('lips from pure milk' = lips wet/soiled/feeding on pure milk); پاک is an adjective qualifying شیر.
segments.s056-g75: به گرد is a locative phrase meaning 'around'; اندر + ش (written as separate tokens) is 'in/within + his' = 'around him'. تیره خاک نژند is a noun phrase 'dark, base dust' (تیره 'dark', خاک 'dust/earth', نژند 'base/ignoble'); Persian allows stacked adjectives/nouns like this to qualify خاک.
segments.s056-g76: به سر is locative 'on/over the head'. برش here is the preposition بر 'on/over' plus enclitic -ش 'his' (written as one token in the text); خورشید is the grammatical subject 'the sun', گشته is the perfect/participial form of شدن 'has become' (here 'had risen/turned'), and بلند is a predicate adjective 'high'. The verb phrase 'خورشید گشته بلند' means 'the sun had grown/become high.'
segments.s056-g77: پلنگش is 'leopard/panther' with the possessive enclitic -ش 'his'. بدی here functions as a comment of ill/evil ('(it) was bad' or 'evil was (his) panther') — the line reads like an exclamation. کاشکی is an optative particle 'if only / would that', introducing a wished-for condition. مام و باب is a colloquial pair 'mother and father' (مام = mother; باب is a dialectal/truncated form for father).
segments.s056-g78: مگر here functions as a rhetorical/exclamatory particle ('did... perhaps?' or 'unless...'), making the following a question: 'did (they) find...?'. سایه‌ای is the indefinite object 'any/a shade' (the -ی ending marks indefiniteness). یافتی is the past tense of یافتن 'to find' (2nd or 3rd singular form; in context it reads as a rhetorical question 'did (you/they) find'). ز is the preposition 'from' and آفتاب is 'the sun.'
segments.s056-g79: فرود آمد is a compound verbal phrase 'fell/descended' or 'alighted' (فرود + آمد). از ابر indicates origin 'from the cloud'. سیمرغ is the subject 'the Simurgh' (the mythical bird). The conjunction و links to چنگ, which here is an instrument 'talon/claw' (preparing for the next verb phrase 'struck with its talon').
segments.s056-g80: بزد is the simple past 'he struck' (here the Simurgh). برگرفتش is برگرفت + enclitic -ش = 'he took/raised him (away)'; the clitic refers back to the infant. از آن گرم سنگ is a prepositional phrase 'from that hot stone' (آن 'that', گرم 'hot', سنگ 'stone'), indicating from where the Simurgh lifted him.
segments.s056-g185: 'مگر' is the conditional 'unless'; 'کاین' (that/this) introduces the thing being spoken of. 'نشیمت' is the noun نشیمن ('seat, place to sit') with a pronominal/possessive ending in classical style meaning 'this/your seat' (poetic economy); 'نیاید' is a negative present/subjunctive form meaning 'does not come / prove not to be'; the phrase 'نیاید به کار' is an idiom 'prove of no use'. Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect (demonstrative + noun + negative verb + 'به کار').
segments.s056-g186: 'یکی' is the indefinite/number 'one' (i.e. a single trial). 'آزمایش کن' is an imperative: 'perform/do a test' — 'کن' is the imperative of کردن. 'از روزگار' literally 'from/of the time/fate' = 'at the hands of fate' or 'by fortune'; the object phrase precedes the verb here, a common poetic order.
segments.s056-g187: 'ابا' (reluctance/refusal) is used here in an elliptical, poetic way meaning 'do not be reluctant' or 'have no scruple'; 'خویشتن' ('oneself') emphasizes that hesitation is internal. 'بر' ('upon/on') + 'یکی پر من' ('one of my feathers') gives the target ('upon one feather of mine'); the phrase literally reads 'reluctance for oneself upon one feather of mine' — the sense is 'do not shrink from taking one of my feathers.' The line uses compact, archaic possessive patterns (noun + personal pronoun) common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s056-g188: 'خجسته' is an adjective 'auspicious/blessed' placed before the copula; 'بود' is the past copula 'was'. 'سایه فر من' is a compound: 'سایه' (shade/protection) modified by 'فر' (royal glory/splendour) with the possessive 'من' ('my') — 'the shade/protection of my glory'. The clause states a past quality: 'the protection of my glory was auspicious.'
segments.s056-g189: گرت is a conditional particle 'if'. هیچ before a noun here has the sense 'any/ever'. سختی is 'hardship, trouble'. به روی آوردند is a verbal phrase where آورند (3rd plural) is used in a potential/conditional sense 'they bring/inflict'; به روی here marks the target ('upon the face/of you') — together 'they bring any hardship upon you'.
segments.s056-g190: ور is an alternative conditional 'or if'. از introduces the content/topic 'about/concerning'. نیک و بد = 'good and bad'. گفت و گوی is the paired nouns for 'speech/talk/conversation' (گفت and گوی used together to mean reported talk). آورند here again is 3rd‑person plural 'they bring/relate/report' — 'if they bring/relate talk about good and bad.'
segments.s056-g191: بر آتش is a locative phrase 'on/upon the fire'. برافگن is an archaic/poetic verbal form meaning 'set ablaze / kindle / throw on the fire' — here used as a past/action in the narrative (plural agent understood from preceding clauses). یکی پر من = 'one feather of mine' (یکی = 'one/a single', پر = 'feather', من = possessive 'my' following the noun in classical word order). The whole clause: 'they set one of my feathers on the fire.'
segments.s056-g192: ببینی is a 2nd‑person form of 'to see' used here with future/optative force — 'you will see' or 'you shall see'. هم 'also'. اندر زمان = 'in the time / in due time'. فر من = 'my far' where فر is the technical classical Persian word for royal glory/splendour and من is the possessive 'my' (postpositive). Together: 'you will also, in time, see my glory.'
segments.s056-g137: گر is the conditional particle ‘if’ at the start. The clause follows a classical Persian order: آهو (subject) + است (copula) + بر مرد (prepositional phrase ‘on/over the man’) + موی سپید (noun + adjective ‘white hair’). سپید qualifies موی; here it means ‘white/gray‑haired.’
segments.s056-g138: تو را marks ‘you’ as the topical/affected person. ریش و سر (beard and head) is the compound subject; گشت is the past/inchoative of گشتن ‘became/turned’ (here ‘have become’). چون introduces a simile ‘like,’ and خنگ بید is a noun phrase ‘dazed/stunned willow’ with خنگ as descriptive. The whole clause states a resulting state for you.
segments.s056-g139: پس here functions as a discourse connective ‘then/therefore.’ از آفریننده (= ‘from/of the Creator’) is the object of the adjectival predicate بیزار; شو is the 2nd‑person imperative of شدن ‘become’ giving the command ‘be averse/loath’ (i.e. ‘turn away’).
segments.s056-g140: که introduces the reason/cause here (‘for/because’). در تنت = ‘in your body’ (تنت is تن + enclitic suffix -ت ‘your’). هر روز is the temporal adverbial ‘every day.’ رنگی‌ست is a present copular phrase ‘is of a color / is colored,’ and نو ‘new’ modifies رنگی‌ست. The clause gives the reason why one should be averse.
segments.s056-g141: پسر‌گر is an archaic agentive noun (‘sire / begetter of sons’) used as the subject. به نزدیک تو is a prepositional phrase ‘near you’; بود is the past copula ‘was’; خوار is a predicative adjective ‘despised/worthless.’ The clause describes a past state: ‘(the sire/son‑maker) was despised when near you.’
segments.s056-g142: کنون ‘now’ sets present time. هست is the present copula ‘is.’ پرورده is a passive/participial adjective ‘reared/nurtured’ used predicatively with هست, and کردگار (‘the Maker’) is the agent understood to have performed the nurturing. Together: ‘now he is reared by the Creator.’
segments.s056-g143: کز is an archaic relative/comparative particle here introducing the comparison ‘than whom/than which.’ او is the third‑person pronoun (referring back to the Creator); مهربان‌تر is the comparative ‘kinder’; ورا = به + را (here ‘to him’) marks the beneficiary; دایه نیست is a negative existential statement ‘there is no nurse.’ The construction says ‘there is no nurse more kind to him than (He).’
segments.s056-g144: تو را is topical/emphatic ‘as for you’; خود is emphatic ‘yourself/indeed.’ به مهر اندرون مایه is a noun phrase ‘an inner store/resource of affection’ (اندرون ‘inner’ before مایه ‘resource’), and نیست negates it. The clause asserts that you do not possess an internal reserve of love or affection.
segments.s056-g153: نشیمی از او برکشیده بلند: literal word order is poetic (object/phrase before verb). نشیمی functions as the thing raised (a seat/place); از او marks source/material or 'out of it'; برکشیده is a past participle/perfect of برداشتن/برکشیدن 'has raised, lifted up' and serves as the verbal predicate; بلند is an adjective postpositive qualifying the seat. The whole phrase reads 'he raised from it a high/lofty seat.'
segments.s056-g154: که ناید ز کیوان بر او بر گزند: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that/so that'); ناید is the classical negative-present/presentive form 'does not come' (i.e. 'no (such) thing comes/occurs'); ز کیوان = 'from Kayvān (Saturn)'; بر او = 'upon it/against it'; گزند is the noun 'harm, injury'. Together: 'so that no harm from Kayvān may come upon it.' Note Persian permits the noun گزند without an explicit verb 'aforefall' and places particles (بر) before it for idiomatic effect.
segments.s056-g155: فرو برده از شیز و صندل عمود: فرو برده is the compound verb فرو بردن in perfect/past participle 'has thrust in/inserted'; عمود is the direct object 'a pillar/column'; از شیز و صندل gives the material ('of shiz and sandalwood'), with از introducing the source/material. Word order places the verb before or after the material phrase in poetry; overall meaning: 'he had inserted a pillar of shiz and sandalwood.'
segments.s056-g156: یک اندر دگر ساخته چوب عود: یک = 'one/another'; اندر دگر = 'into/inside another' (literally 'in/among another'); ساخته is the past participle/verb 'made'; چوب عود = 'wood of oud/agarwood' specifying material. The clause says he made another inside it, of agarwood. The verb (ساخته) is the action and the material phrase follows it.
segments.s056-g157: بدان سنگ خارا نگه کرد سام: بدان here is the fused prepositional element 'to/at that' (به + آن) introducing the object; سنگ خارا = 'flinty/hard stone' (سنگ + the adjective خارا); نگه کرد is the verbal phrase 'kept a gaze / looked' (کرد is the past of کردن used with نگه 'gaze'); سام, the proper name, appears after the verb for emphasis (typical poetic order). Overall: 'Sam looked toward that flinty rock.'
segments.s056-g158: بدان هیبت مرغ و هول کنام: بدان 'at that' (see above); هیبت = 'awe/majestic appearance' describing the rock; مرغ و هول کنام is a compact, verbless descriptive phrase: مرغ (literally 'bird', used metaphorically for fluttering) and هول ('terror') placed with کنام ('in the bosom') to mean 'a fluttering and terror in the breast.' Persian poetry commonly omits a copula, so the phrase describes Sam's inner state provoked by that majesty.
segments.s056-g159: یکی کاخ بد تارک اندر سماک: یکی = 'a certain/one'; کاخ = 'palace'; بد here is an evaluative adjective 'grim/strange/ill-omened'; تارک is a poetic/archaic word whose exact nuance is obscure in this context (the compound may describe the palace's aspect or condition); اندر سماک = 'in/within Samak' or 'upon Samak' (Samaak is treated as a proper name/place). The phrase presents the palace and its location/association before qualifying how it was made.
segments.s056-g160: نه از دست رنج و نه از آب و خاک: a paired negation 'neither ... nor ...'. از دست رنج = 'by the hand of toil' (i.e. by human labor); از آب و خاک = 'from water and earth' (i.e. from earthly materials). The verb 'is/was' is omitted (copula), a normal poetic device: the palace was neither the product of labor nor of earthly materials.
segments.s056-g89: This clause uses an archaic poetic word order with the objects before the verb: شگفتی and مهر are the things 'cast' toward او (the target). فگندند is the past, 3rd person plural of 'to throw/cast' (they hurled/bestowed). بر او (preposition + pronoun) marks the recipient. In effect it means 'they directed wonder and love toward him.'
segments.s056-g90: بماندند is past, 3rd person plural 'they remained' or 'they stood'; خیره is a predicative adjective/adverb meaning 'staring' or 'dumbstruck'. بدان is an archaic prepositional phrase 'به + آن' = 'at that' (here written as ب + دان); خوب‌چهر is the noun 'fair‑faced one' that the clause refers to. Word order places the state (خیره) before the locative/target phrase (بدان خوب‌چهر).
segments.s056-g91: شکاری is a noun meaning 'hunter' or 'predatory one' and serves as the subject; که introduces a relative (or descriptive) clause 'who/that'; نازک‌تر is the comparative adjective 'thinner/more delicate' modifying آن ('that one'); برگزید is the past, 3rd person singular 'he chose'. The object آن follows the adjective (typical poetic order): 'the hunter who chose that which was thinner.'
segments.s056-g92: Here که introduces a causal/explanatory clause 'for/because'. بی‌شیر is an adjective 'without milk' (literally 'milkless'); مهمان is 'guest' (used here for the suckling/visitor at the breast or a parasitic guest); همی is a continuous adverb 'continually/always'; خون is the object 'blood'; مزید here functions as a verb/adjectival verb 'increased/kept drawing' (poetic usage meaning 'drew more'). The clause explains the reason the thinner one was chosen: the milkless guest was ever taking more blood.
segments.s056-g93: بدین گونه is an adverbial phrase 'in this manner/in this way' (ب + دین/این in older orthography + گونه). تا + روزگاری دراز is a temporal expression 'until / for a long time' — تا here marks the extent/duration ('for'). Together they set the temporal frame: 'in this way, for a long time.'
segments.s056-g94: برآورد is a past verb meaning 'brought about/caused/accomplished'; داننده ('the knower' or 'one who knows') is the agent; بگشاد is the perfective 'opened/revealed' (here governing راز 'the secret'). The sequence presents two related actions by the knower: he effected something and opened/revealed the secret.
segments.s056-g95: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; آن کودک خرد is the noun phrase 'that little child' (خرد here = 'small/young'); پر مایه is a compound adjectival phrase meaning 'full of feathers / plumed' (پر = feather, مایه = substance) and گشت is the past 3rd singular 'became' — 'when that small child became plumed/grew feathers.'
segments.s056-g96: بر آن کوه is a locative prepositional phrase 'upon that mountain'; بر روزگاری is another prepositional temporal phrase 'for a time'; گذشت is an intransitive past verb 'passed' or idiomatically 'spent [time]'. Together: 'he spent/passed some time on that mountain.' Note the double بر: first marks location, second marks temporal extent.
segments.s056-g105: چنان is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' and sets the manner of what follows; دید is the simple past 'he saw' (3rd sg.). در خواب is a prepositional phrase 'in a dream' (خواب here = 'dream'); کز is the archaic fused form of که + از meaning 'that/from' and introduces the origin phrase 'from the Indians' (هندوان). Word order is typical classical Persian: adverb → verb → circumstance → subordinate introduced by کز.
segments.s056-g106: یکی here works as 'a certain' (indefinite). مرد is the noun 'man' and بر introduces the rider-on relation ('on'). تازی qualifies اسپ to mean 'Arabian' (here specifically an Arabian steed), and دوان is a participial/adjectival form meaning 'galloping' or 'running' that modifies اسپ (the participle follows the noun it qualifies in this line). The clause lacks an explicit copula — common in Persian narration — so the sequence describes 'a certain man on a galloping Arabian horse.'
segments.s056-g107: ورا is an object pronoun 'to him' placed before the verb for emphasis; مژده دادی is the past tense 'you gave (good) news' (دادی = 2nd person singular past of دادن). به فرزند او is a beneficiary/recipient phrase 'for/to his son' introduced by به. The sequence means 'you gave him the tidings concerning his son.'
segments.s056-g108: بر آن برز is a locative/instrumental phrase: بر = 'on/upon', آن = 'that', برز = 'lofty/high' (here describing a trunk or stem). شاخ برومند او is a noun phrase 'his noble branch' with the adjective برومند following the noun (adjective after noun is common), and او marks the possessor 'his.' Together it expresses that 'a noble branch of his will spring on that high trunk.'
segments.s056-g109: چو is the conjunction 'when'; بیدار شد is a past-clause 'he became awake' (i.e., he awoke) where شد is the past auxiliary. موبدان را uses را as the definite/direct-object marker; بخواند is past 3rd-person singular of خواندن with the meaning 'he called/summoned' here (classical narrative past form).
segments.s056-g110: از این در is a prepositional phrase meaning 'about this matter' (از ... در = concerning/regarding). سخن چند گونه is 'speech/words of several kinds' (چند = 'several'; گونه = 'kind, manner'), and براند is the classical verb راندن used with سخن to mean 'to utter/put forth' (here 3rd sg.), so the whole means 'he uttered/said several kinds of speech about this.'
segments.s056-g111: چه is the interrogative 'what'; گویید here functions as the verb 'you say' (2nd person plural) in the classical form 'گویید' = 'do you say' and opens the question. گفت is a nominal/summary form meaning 'said/talk' used with اندر این داستان 'in this story' (اندر = 'in/regarding'); the whole line asks 'What do you have to say about this tale?'
segments.s056-g112: خردتان is 'your judgment/wisdom' with the possessive plural suffix -تان; بر این هست is the copular construction 'is regarding this' (بر = 'on/regarding', هست = 'is'). هم‌داستان is a predicate adjective meaning 'of the same mind/agreeing', so the question asks whether their judgment is in agreement about the matter.
segments.s056-g145: به خواب اندرون is a locative phrase placed before the verb: به + خواب (sleep) + اندرون (within/inner quarters) — literally 'in/within sleep'. برخروشید is the past tense (3rd sing.) of خروشیدن with the prefix بر- giving an upward/eruptive sense: 'broke into a roar / sprang up with a roar'. سام (proper name) appears after the verb, a common poetic order (verb + subject).
segments.s056-g146: چو introduces a simile/temporal clause ('as' / 'when'); here it functions as 'as when' or 'like when'. شیر ژیان is a nominal phrase where ژیان (literally 'life') is used adjectivally/poetically to mean a living/ fierce lion. کاندر آید is an older/poetic sequence meaning 'comes into/within' (کاندر = 'in which/therein' + آید = 'comes'), and به دام completes the idea 'into the trap/snare.'
segments.s056-g147: چو بیدار شد is a temporal clause 'when he became awake / when he awoke' (چو = 'when', بیدار شد = past 'became awake'). بخردان را بخواند: بخردان ('the wise ones') is the definite object marked by را, and بخواند here is the narrative/past-form verb 'he called / he summoned' (classical poetic past form), so the clause means 'he summoned the wise.'
segments.s056-g148: سران سپه را is a genitive grouping (سران = 'chiefs/heads' + سپه = 'army') with را marking them as the object. همه is an adverbial 'all' (emphasizing totality) and برنشاند is the past (3rd sing.) of نشاندن with the prefix بر- giving a sense 'set/placed (on seats) / caused to sit' — so 'he seated all the army-chiefs.' The subject remains implicit (Sam).
segments.s056-g149: بیامد is simple past 'he came'. دمان (literary) means the skirt/foot of a slope (here 'foothill' or 'foot/skirts of the mountain') and is the location. سوی آن کوهسار = 'toward that mountain' (سوی = 'toward', آن کوهسار = 'that mountain'). The verb precedes the location phrases in this poetic order.
segments.s056-g150: که introduces a relative/ subordinate clause ('which' / 'where'). افگندگان را is the (marked) object 'the throwers' (those who cast/throw, likely attackers or missile-throwers). کند is the classical-narrative present/past auxiliary/verb 'makes/does' (in epic Persian, کند often serves as simple past/narrative). خواستار here functions as a predicate/adjectival noun 'seeking/aspired' or 'one who seeks.' The clause therefore expresses that at/at which he made himself/the place to seek the throwers — idiomatically: 'where he sought the throwers.'
segments.s056-g151: سر اندر ثریا is a locative/illustrative phrase: سر = 'summit/top', اندر = 'in/within', ثریا = the Pleiades (used poetically as a high place). یکی کوه = 'one mountain' (numeric determiner preceding the noun in poetic order). دید is the past 'he saw' placed at the end, so the whole segment reads 'he saw a single mountain's summit within the Pleiades.'
segments.s056-g152: که introduces a relative clause modifying that mountain. گفتی here is a formulaic 'it is said / as people say' (classical usage of گفتی to introduce a reported or proverbial remark). ستاره is 'star' (celestial), and بخواهد is the present/future-form of خواستن used here as 'would/will/wished to' (expressing desire or potential). کشید is the past of کشیدن 'to pull/drag' (poetic; here 'a star wished to draw/pull' the mountain). The clause therefore reports a saying that a star (mythically) desired to pull it.
segments.s056-g97: This clause uses the indefinite/poetic particle 'یکی' (here 'a certain') before 'مرد' to introduce a single remarkable man. 'شد' is the past of شدن meaning 'came to be / appeared.' 'چون' is a simile marker 'like/as,' and the second 'یکی' introduces the simile 'one who' (someone); 'زاد' here is used in the sense of birth/origin (classic poetic use, effectively 'was born'), and 'سرو' is a metaphorical noun 'cypress' (a tall, graceful tree) applied to a person. Word order is typical Persian: topic/indefinite phrase + predicate (شد) then a comparative clause introduced by چون.
segments.s056-g98: 'برش' here is actually بر + -ش (the preposition 'on/upon' with the enclitic third-person pronoun) meaning 'upon him' (an enclitic construction common in poetry). 'کوه' is literal 'mountain' and pairs with the omitted adjective سیمین ('silver') in the line; 'میانش' is میان + -ش, 'his middle' or 'its middle/centre' (the enclitic shows possession), 'چو' again marks a simile 'as/like', and 'غرو' is a poetic noun meaning 'sunset/twilight.' The clause therefore images a 'silver mountain' on/over him and compares his middle/profile to the evening. Note: the lexical entry given for 'برش' in the word list does not reflect the enclitic reading; here it is a preposition + pronoun.
segments.s056-g99: 'نشانش' is نشان + -ش (the noun 'sign/mark' with the third-person possessive enclitic), here meaning his mark or fame. 'پراگنده' is an adjective 'scattered/widespread'; 'شد' is the past copula (literally 'became/was') used to form 'was scattered / spread'; 'در' is the preposition 'in' and 'جهان' is 'the world.' The word order places the possessed noun first and then the verbal predicate 'پراگنده شد در جهان' ('spread in the world').
segments.s056-g100: This clause states a general truth: 'بد و نیک' ('bad and good') are coordinated by 'و.' 'هرگز' is a negative-frequency adverb 'never,' and 'نماند' is the past subjunctive/negative-perfect sense of ماندن meaning 'did not remain / never remained.' 'نهان' is an adjective 'hidden.' The negation is carried by 'هرگز' governing 'نماند' to mean 'never remained hidden.'
segments.s056-g101: 'به' marks the indirect object/goal ('to/for'); here it introduces the person receiving the news: 'به سام نریمان' = 'to Sam Nariman.' 'رسید' is the past tense of رسیدن meaning 'reached/came to,' and 'آگهی' is the noun 'news/information' functioning as the subject of رسید. Persian places the experiencer/recipient (به + name) before the verb in such constructions.
segments.s056-g102: 'از' here introduces the source or topic of the news and is best read as 'concerning/of' (not purely locative). 'آن' is the demonstrative 'that'; 'نیک‌پی' is a compound adjective 'well-born/virtuous'; 'پور' is 'son/descendant' (used as a noun here), and 'با فرهی' is a prepositional phrase 'with [one] of noble/cultured bearing' (با + adjective) describing the son. The whole phrase 'از آن نیک‌پی پور با فرهی' thus specifies 'about that well-born son of noble bearing.'
segments.s056-g103: 'شبی' is an adverbial noun 'one night.' 'از' here functions causally ('because of' / 'with') linking the following noun phrase, and 'شبان' is 'shepherds' (the scene or company where the sleeping occurs). 'داغ دل' is a compound meaning 'the burning grief of the heart' (داغ = burning grief; دل = heart), 'خفته' is the past participle/adjective 'asleep/laid down,' and 'بود' is the past copula 'was' (forming 'had been asleep' in context). The phrase orders the time adverbial first, then the circumstantial phrase of cause/location, then the state ('خفته بود').
segments.s056-g104: 'ز' is the poetic variant of 'از' meaning 'from' or 'because of' and here introduces the cause/subject: 'ز کار زمانه' = 'by/for the affairs of the time.' 'کار' means 'affair/deed/affairs,' and 'زمانه' is 'the age/time' often used for the world's affairs or fortune. 'برآشفته' is a compound adjective meaning 'upset/agitated/distressed' (بر- prefixed participle), and the clause ends with 'بود' (past 'was'). The whole clause describes his emotional disturbance caused by the state of the times.
segments.s056-g113: هر آنکس که introduces a generalizing relative clause: ‘whomever/ everyone who…’. بودنـد is the past plural form of بودن (they were), agreeing with the understood plural referents (‘those people’). پیر و جوان are predicate adjectives (‘old and young’) placed after the copula; the whole segment means ‘all those who were old and young’ (i.e. everyone, young and old).
segments.s056-g114: زبان here means ‘speech/utterance’ and is the thing opened; برگشادند is the past plural verb ‘they opened’ (classical/poetic past). بر + پهلوان marks the addressee (‘to/toward the hero’). The clause omits an explicit reporting verb like ‘said’ because برگشودن ‘to open the tongue’ idiomatically means ‘to speak’.
segments.s056-g115: که begins a content/relative clause (‘who/that’ or ‘(they spoke) that…’). The repeated بر is a poetic parallelism meaning ‘on/upon’ (بر سنگ و بر خاک = ‘on rock and on soil’). شیر و پلنگ are coordinated nouns ('lion and panther') being presented as the things discussed; word order is inverted for poetic rhythm (prepositional phrases precede the animals).
segments.s056-g116: چه modifies ماهی and is interrogative/exclamatory ‘what (kind of)’. به دریا درون is a locative phrase ‘in/into the sea’ (به…درون = ‘in the waters’), with درون ‘inside’ after the place for emphasis. با نهنگ ‘with the whale’ indicates accompaniment. The clause has no explicit finite verb here because it continues the earlier main verb (‘opened their mouths / said’) — so this is the content of what was said.
segments.s056-g117: همه is a universal subject ‘all / everyone’. بچه را is the definite object with the object marker را (‘the child’). پروراننده‌اند is a present/habitual plural predicate formed from the agentive stem plus -اند: ‘they rear/they have reared’. The clause follows S (همه) – O (بچه را) – V (پروراننده‌اند) order.
segments.s056-g118: ستایش ‘praise’ precedes the verb and functions as the thing being brought; به یزدان is an indirect-object/dative phrase ‘to God’. رساننده‌اند is again the present/habitual plural form ‘they bring/deliver’ (agentive stem + -اند). The whole means they render praise to God (they bring praise unto God).
segments.s056-g119: تو is the second-person subject ‘you’. پیمان نیکی is a genitival phrase ‘the pledge/covenant of goodness’ (پیمان = oath/pact, نیکی = goodness). دهش here is a noun related to giving (‘act of giving’ or ‘grant’), so the phrase denotes the pledged gift/promise of good. بشکنی is the second-person singular subjunctive/present (classical form) of شکستن ‘to break’ and expresses the act ‘you break’ (here conditional—‘if you break…’—the protasis is implied).
segments.s056-g120: چنان is an adverb ‘thus/so’ indicating manner/result. بی‌گنه is an adjective ‘innocent’ modifying بچه (the child), and بچه را marks the definite object with را. بفگنی is an archaic/uncertain verb form (listed as an archaic verb in the lexicon) that takes بچه را as its object; in context it functions as the result clause (‘you will thus [do harm to / wrong] the innocent child’), so the translator renders it as a verb meaning ‘do wrong/ harm’. The clause therefore states the consequence of breaking the pledge.
segments.s056-g161: ره بر شدن جست is a compact verbal phrase: ره (way) + بر (on/upon) + شدن (to get/become on) + جست (past of جهیدن, 'leapt'). Together it means 'he leapt to/get onto the way.' The conjoined و plus کی بود راه begins a rhetorical question ('and when was there a way...'), so راه (way) is carried over into the next segment as the thing being queried.
segments.s056-g162: دد و دام را بر چنان جایگاه is the continuation/completion of the question begun in the previous segment: 'a way (راه) for دد و دام (malice/hostility and trap).' را is the definite/object marker emphasizing 'دد و دام' as the object; بر چنان جایگاه ('on/onto such a place') locates that path. Word order (object + بر + place) is poetic but standard in classical Persian.
segments.s056-g163: ابر آفریننده کرد آفرین has the subject (ابر, 'cloud') followed by آفریننده (here an epithet/apposition 'the Creator' or 'praiser') and the verb کرد ('did/made') plus the exclamation آفرین ('Afarin' = 'bravo/praise'). Grammatically, کرد takes آفرین as its object/complement: 'the cloud (or the Creator’s cloud) uttered “Afarin.”' This is a terse, poetic word order.
segments.s056-g164: بمالید رخسارگان بر زمین is a simple past verb phrase: بمالید (third-person past, 'rubbed/anointed') with رخسارگان ('faces/countenances') as the object and بر زمین ('upon the earth') as the locative. The subject (the cloud) is carried over from the previous segment; the verb's -ید ending is the classical past form.
segments.s056-g165: همی گفت is an iterative/habitual construction: همی (iterative particle, 'kept / was continually') + گفت (here functioning as 'said'). کای is a vocative particle 'O' addressing someone, and برتر از جایگاه is a comparative phrase 'higher/superior in station.' Word order places the vocative and comparative expression immediately after the reporting verb, as is common in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s056-g166: ز روشن‌روان و ز خورشید و ماه uses the poetic preposition ز (here comparative 'than' / 'from') twice to link the item being compared to two sets: روشن‌روان ('the bright‑souled' — a compound epithet) and خورشید و ماه ('the sun and the moon'). The repeated ز makes a parallel comparative: 'loftier than the bright‑souled, and than the sun and moon.'
segments.s056-g167: گر این کودک از پاک پشت من است is a conditional clause: گر ('if') + این کودک ('this child') + از پاکِ پشتِ من ('from my pure back/stock' — a poetic idiom meaning 'of my pure lineage') + است ('is'). پشت here denotes lineage/ancestry in poetic usage. The clause sets up a hypothetical about the child's origin.
segments.s056-g168: نه از تخم بد گوهر آهرمن است is the negated apodosis: نه ('not') + از تخم ('from the seed/of the stock') + بد گوهر ('ill‑essenced' or 'evil‑gem' — here 'bad essence') + آهرمن ('Ahriman', the devil) + است ('is'). The particle نه negates the child's being of Ahriman's seed; گوهر functions as 'essence' in this moral/poetic sense.
segments.s056-g217: منم is the poetic/colloquial contraction of من هستم ('I am') with the clitic -م marking first person. کم‌ترین is the superlative form (کم + -ترین) meaning 'least' or 'humblest' and modifies the following noun. بنده یزدان‌پرست is a noun phrase where بنده is a humble self-reference/'servant/devotee' and یزدان‌پرست follows as a compound adjective ('God-worshipping/pious'), so the whole phrase reads 'I am the humblest God-worshipping servant.'
segments.s056-g218: از آن پس که is a temporal phrase meaning 'since the time that' (از + demonstrative آن + پس + relative که). آوردمت is past tense with the object clitic -ت ('I brought you') and the final -م ('I'); in older/poetic Persian this form appears without an auxiliary. باز here functions adjectivally ('back' / 'again' / 'open' in older usage) modifying دست ('hand'); together باز دست is literal 'back/open hand' and in context indicates that the bringing-back occurred into the speaker's control/hand. The clause therefore gives the temporal frame 'since I brought you back (into my hand).'
segments.s056-g219: پذیرفته‌ام is the perfect/resultant form 'I have accepted' (پذیرفته + -ام), used here to report a settled resolution received from a divine source. از indicates source ('from'), and خدای بزرگ is the prepositional phrase 'the Great God' (خدای = God, بزرگ = great) with straightforward noun + adjective order.
