segments.s056-g220: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing the content of what was accepted. دل بر تو is a phrase where دل ('heart') takes the preposition بر + person (تو) to mean 'a heart toward you' or 'feeling for you.' هرگز ندارم uses هرگز ('never') plus the present/stative verb ندارم ('I do not have' / 'I shall not have') to express a permanent negation. سترگ follows as a predicate/adjectival notion ('proud/exalted' in context); the whole clause reads 'that my heart will never be proud toward you.'
segments.s056-g221: بجویم is a first-person singular present/subjunctive form of جویدن/جستن used here as 'I will seek' / 'I seek' (poetic optative/intentional sense). هوای تو uses هوای (literally 'air') idiomatically to mean 'desire/favour/affection' and is followed by the possessor تو. از نیک و بد ('from/in good and bad') uses از to indicate 'in regard to' or 'through both' moral conditions; نیک و بد are the paired nouns 'good and bad.'
segments.s056-g222: از این پس is a fixed temporal phrase 'from this time on / henceforth.' چه خواهی تو places the interrogative چه ('what') before خواهی (2nd-person form of خواستن) for 'what will you want/ask for?' Note the poetic/older word order with the pronoun تو after the verb. چونان سزد is an impersonal clause: چونان ('so/thus/as such') + سزد (impersonal verb 'it befits/it is fitting'), yielding 'which would be fitting.' Together the question asks what the addressee will wish that would be proper from now on.
segments.s056-g223: تنش را is 'his body' (تن + possessive clitic ش, followed by the object marker را to mark it as definite/direct object). یکی here functions as an indefinite numeral/adjective 'a single' or 'one.' پهلوانی قبای is a genitive/attributive sequence where پهلوانی supplies a 'heroic/warrior' quality to قبا ('robe'), so قبای is the clothing item being applied to the body.
segments.s056-g224: بپوشید is a past narrative form 'he/they clothed (him)' (ب- prefix + پوشید), used here in the story's past tense. و simply links the two actions. از کوه introduces origin/location 'from the mountain,' and بگزارد پای is a classical phrasing where بگزارد (past of گزارْدَن) 'placed/put' governs پای ('foot'), so 'he set his foot down/placed his foot off the mountain'—an idiomatic way to say he descended or alighted from the mountain.
segments.s056-g201: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; پروازش is پرواز 'flight' with the possessive -ش ('its/his'), so ز پروازش functions as an adverbial phrase 'from its flight'. آورد is a simple past 3rd-person singular 'brought' (classical narrative past). نزد پدر is the goal phrase 'to the presence of the father' (نزد = 'in the presence of/near'), placed after the verb as the destination.
segments.s056-g202: رسیده is a past participle / perfect-like form 'having reached, arrived'. به + زیر gives direction 'to under / beneath', so به زیر modifies رسیده. برش موی سر is a genitive group: برش 'slice/tuft/section' of موی سر 'the hair of the head' — together 'beneath the tuft/lock of the head's hair'. The whole phrase means 'having arrived beneath the tuft of hair on the head.'
segments.s056-g203: تنش is تن 'body' plus the enclitic -ش 'his' (so 'his body'). پیلوار is an adjective/adverb meaning 'continuously/entirely' modifying تنش. و links to the next clause. به رخ 'on/at (his) face' is a prepositional phrase and چون بهار is a simile 'like spring' describing his face. Word order places descriptive phrases after the noun as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s056-g204: چون here functions temporally/causally 'when/as' (classical use meaning 'when'); بدیدش is the past simple 'he saw him' with the enclitic object -ش. بنالید is past tense of نالیدن 'to lament/cry' (classical past form, with the prefixed ب- often used in older Persian). زار is an adverb/adjective 'wretchedly/mournfully' modifying the lament.
segments.s056-g205: فروبرد is a compound of the prefix فرو- 'down' plus برد (from بردن) and here means 'lowered/put down' — so فرو برد سر = 'lowered the head' or 'bowed his head'. پیش سیمرغ is the locative 'before/in the presence of the Simorgh' (پیش = 'in the presence of'). زود is an adverb 'quickly/at once' modifying the verb.
segments.s056-g206: نیایش is a noun 'prayer/supplication'. همی is the classical continuous/emphatic particle (here signalling the ongoing or habitual action 'was praying / did pray'). بآفرین is ب- + آفرین 'with praise' or 'uttering “bravo”' (an adverbial phrase). برفزود is a classical past 3rd-singular of فزودن 'to increase/bestow' (here 'bestowed' or 'gave'), so the clause reads 'he prayed and, with praise, bestowed (a boon).'
segments.s056-g207: سراپای is an adverb/adjective 'from head to foot, entirely'; کودک is 'the child'. همی again is the classical particle marking ongoing or emphatic aspect. بنگرید is a classical past/simple narrative form of نگریستن 'to look, to gaze' (here 'he looked over/inspected'). The phrase means 'he looked over the child's whole body.'
segments.s056-g208: همی is emphatic/continuative. تاج و تخت 'crown and throne' is the coordinated noun phrase; کئی is a genitive marker 'of Kai / Kayanian' (i.e. belonging to the Kayanian line). را is the definite object marker highlighting that تاج و تخت کئی is what is in view. سزید is the past of سزا/سزیدن 'to befit / to deserve' (here used to mean 'befitted him' or 'was fitting/appropriate'), so the clause says that the crown and throne of Kai were fitting for him.
segments.s056-g209: بر is the preposition 'on/upon' that governs the following nominal phrases; the phrase lists two emblems in inverted, poetic order linked by و ('and'). بازوی شیر = 'the (arm) of a lion' and خورشید روی = 'the sun on (his) face' (روی = face placed after خورشید as a postmodifier). The possessor ('his') is omitted and understood from context, a common poetic ellipsis.
segments.s056-g210: دل پهلوان is a genitive phrase 'the hero's heart' with the ezāfe omitted (typical in classical poetry). دست شمشیر = 'the hand (or hilt) of the sword' and جوی is used adjectivally/participially 'seeking' (i.e. 'seeking the sword' or 'longing for the sword'); the verb 'to be' is omitted, so the line reads as a verbless clause describing desire.
segments.s056-g211: سپیدش is the adjective 'white' with the enclitic -ش ('his/its'), here functioning like 'his white (one)' and referring to the eyelashes; مژه دیدگان = 'the eyelashes of the eyes' (classical genitive without an explicit ezāfe). قیرگون is a postpositive adjective 'pitch‑black' modifying دیدگان.
segments.s056-g212: چو 'when/as' introduces a temporal/simile clause. بسد is an archaic/poetic verbal form meaning 'becomes' or 'is suffused' (here 'grows/turns into'); لب و رخ = 'lips and face/cheeks' joined by و; به مانند خون = 'as/like blood.' Together: 'when his lips and face became like blood.'
segments.s056-g213: دل سام = 'the heart of Sam' (genitive; no ezāfe). شد is the past tense 'became.' چون ... بهشت برین is a simile 'like the highest paradise' — برین is the adjectival formation ('-ین' suffix) giving 'supreme/highest.' The clause says Sam's heart was transformed into bliss.
segments.s056-g214: بر آن پاک فرزند is a prepositional phrase 'upon/for that pure child' (آن = that; پاک فرزند = 'pure child' or 'pure offspring'). کرد آفرین is a light-verb construction where کرد 'made/did' takes آفرین ('praise/acclamation') as its object — 'he praised/expressed acclaim for that pure child.'
segments.s056-g215: به من = 'to me' (dative), and ای پسر is a vocative 'O son' addressing the addressee; گفت is the past verb 'said.' دل نرم کن is an imperative: دل = '(your) heart' (possessive is omitted), نرم = 'soft/gentle,' and کن is the imperative 'do' — 'soften your heart.' The quoted imperative is embedded after گفت.
segments.s056-g216: گذشته مکن = 'do not (mēkon) the past' i.e. 'do not dwell on/recall the past' — مکن is the negative imperative 'do not.' یاد is 'memory/remembering' serving as the object ('do not remember'). و links to the next imperative دل گرم‌کن = 'make/keep the heart warm' (an idiom meaning 'cheer/encourage yourself').
segments.s056-g193: که is the subordinating/complementizer 'that' introducing the clause. در زیر پرت is a prepositional phrase 'in/under (the) پرت' placed before the verb for emphasis. بپرورده‌ام is first‑person singular perfect ('I have nurtured/raised'): ب- (verbal prefix) + پرورده (past participle) + ام (1sg enclitic).
segments.s056-g194: This clause states a second, related fact. ابا here is uncertain in the supplied sense list (likely a proper name or an archaic/formal particle in context) and does not match the listed senses. بچگانت is 'your children' (بچگان + ـت possessive), and برآورده‌ام is a perfective first‑person ('I have brought up / provided for / raised'), where the verb carries the main predication.
segments.s056-g195: همان گه is a temporal phrase 'at that moment' (همان = 'that/the same', گه = 'time/occasion'). بیایم is subjunctive/temporal 'when I come' (used after temporal particles), and چو ابر سیاه is a simile 'like a black/dark cloud' modifying the coming.
segments.s056-g196: بی‌آزارت is an adjective/phrase 'without harming you / harmless to you' (the final ـت is the 2nd‑person enclitic). آرم is first‑person present/subjunctive 'I will rest / I may rest.' بدین جایگاه is a prepositional phrase 'to/into this place' (بدین = به + این); here ب = 'to/into' and جایگاه = 'place.' (The tokenized دین is the 'این' element expressed orthographically here.)
segments.s056-g197: فرامش مکن is a prohibitive construction: فرامش (noun 'forgetfulness') + مکن ('do not') = 'do not forget.' مهر دایه is a genitive phrase 'the nurse's love/affection.' ز دل ('from the heart') specifies source or origin.
segments.s056-g198: که introduces the explanatory/causal clause 'for/because.' در دل = 'in (the) heart.' مرا here marks possession ('my') in the phrase 'in my heart' (the tokenization shows م + را but together convey the possessive/objective relation). مهر تو = 'your love' (تو = possessive 'your'), and دلگسل is a predicate adjective 'heart‑rending,' completing the clause: 'for in my heart your love is heart‑rending.'
segments.s056-g199: دلش کرد is an idiom: دل + ـش (his/her) + کرد ('made/did'), here meaning 'his heart was moved' or 'he was moved/persuaded.' پدرام is the actor; word order is inverted (object/affected phrase before the verb). برداشتش is a past tense verb 'he lifted/took it' with the enclitic ـش = 'it/him/her' as its object.
segments.s056-g200: This clause describes the upward motion: گرازان is unclear in the supplied sense list for this context (the listed senses 'boars' do not fit the action here) and is therefore given as uncertain. به ابر اندر = 'into/onto the cloud' (به = 'to/into', ابر = 'cloud', اندر = 'in/within'), and افراشتش is a past form 'he raised/erected it' (افراشت + ـش 'it').

sentences.s056-s121: They entered the city rejoicing; their heroism grew greater.
sentences.s056-s13: May the day be auspicious for Sam the hero, and may the hearts of his ill‑omened enemies be torn out.
sentences.s056-s15: His body was silver‑like and his face like paradise; on him you will not see a single ugly feature.
sentences.s056-s16: A white lock of hair, like one taken from a gazelle, was his — such was your portion, Namjui.
sentences.s056-s25: What shall I say — is this child a demon? It has the coloring of a leopard, two‑coloured; otherwise it is a fairy.
sentences.s056-s26: Because of this shame I will abandon the land of Iran; I do not wish to be on this soil nor on Afarin.
sentences.s056-s27: He ordered (so); then they took Tash and removed it from that soil and set it far away.
sentences.s056-s28: To the place where the Simurgh had its dwelling; that house was this little one's strange home.
sentences.s056-s33: If I have given you the blood of my heart, I did not place any thanks upon your head.
sentences.s056-s34: That you yourself have seen me and sympathized; my heart would break if you broke away from me.
sentences.s056-s35: When the Simorgh's chick became hungry, it took wing and rose from the pistachio-bush.
sentences.s056-s36: A certain suckling, roaring, was seen; (one) saw the earth like a seething sea.
sentences.s056-s21: If I have committed a grievous sin, or if I have brought the creed of Ahriman.
sentences.s056-s22: May the Creator of the world, for pardon, forgive me secretly.
sentences.s056-s23: My soul twists darkly from shame; hot blood seethes in my heart.
sentences.s056-s24: When the rebels come and ask, what shall I say to them about this child's body?
sentences.s056-s29: They set it on the mountain and returned; a long period then came to pass.
sentences.s056-s30: So heroic‑born and innocent, he could not tell white from black.
sentences.s056-s31: The father cast affection and kinship into contempt and did cruelty to the nursing infant.
sentences.s056-s32: A certain tale was told about this male lion: where he had made the child full with milk.
sentences.s056-s5: From that "moon" of hers there was hope of a child, who was sun-faced and noble.
sentences.s056-s6: He also bore the burden of (the line of) Sam and Nariman; the heavy burden afflicted his body.
sentences.s056-s7: He was separated from his mother for a few days; a beauty like the sun, world‑illuminating.
sentences.s056-s8: In appearance he was so radiant, a shining beloved; yet all his hair was white.
sentences.s056-s61: Now turn toward God in seeking pardon, for He is the guide to goodness.
sentences.s056-s62: When the night grew dark, the thought of sleep came to him; from his thought his heart was filled with haste.
sentences.s056-s63: He saw in a dream that from the mountain of India a tall banner was raised.
sentences.s056-s64: A handsome youth appeared; heavy troops came up from behind his back.
sentences.s056-s9: When the son was born of his mother in that manner, they did not mention Sam for a week.
sentences.s056-s10: In the bedchamber of that famed hero, everyone stood before that little newborn child.
sentences.s056-s11: No one came to Sam the hero; (then) someone said that the elderly man's child had come from a good match.
sentences.s056-s12: There was a certain wet-nurse who, acting like a lion, boldly approached the champion.
sentences.s056-s65: In his left hand was a certain mobad, and to his right a famed man of wisdom.
sentences.s056-s66: One of those two men came before Sam, and he opened his mouth to speak in a stern tone.
sentences.s056-s67: “O man—fearless, with impure counsel—your heart and eyes are washed of the shame of God.”
sentences.s056-s68: Perhaps a bird was your nurse, then what good is this heroism of yours?
sentences.s056-s17: The rider dismounted from Sam's throne and entered the tent toward the fresh spring.
sentences.s056-s18: When he saw his son, whose hair was white, he was completely hopeless of the world.
sentences.s056-s19: He raised his head straight toward the sky, and then cried out to the just-giver.
sentences.s056-s20: O you who are superior to crookedness and deficiency — may what you desired become even better than that.
sentences.s056-s1: Now I will relate an astonishing tale from ancient reports.
sentences.s056-s2: Look — see what game Fate played with my Sam; listen, O son.
sentences.s056-s3: There was not any child of Sam; his heart was a seeker of desire.
sentences.s056-s4: In his inner chamber was a lovely image; her face was of a flower‑petal and her hair of musk.
sentences.s056-s113: He descended from the mountain and sought an upper garment; he desired that very robe that adorned Khosrow.
sentences.s056-s114: The army straightaway came before Sam; they arrived open‑hearted and fortunate.
sentences.s056-s115: The axemen brought an elephant forward; one appeared, enormous like the Nile.
sentences.s056-s116: The war‑drums sounded with the korenai (reed‑pipes), the same golden bells and Indian adornments accompanying them.
sentences.s056-s117: All the horsemen raised a shout; with that joy they set off on the road.
sentences.s056-s118: When at night in the air the banner was unfurled, there appeared that Roman face of Zangi lineage.
